sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 36-year-old female, presents with a history of acid reflux since the age of 12, which has worsened over the past few years.
She reports taking 40 milligrams of omeprazole twice daily, with some improvement, but still experiencing symptoms.
She describes the sensation of burning in her chest and throat, with no difficulty swallowing, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, changes in bowel movements, or rectal bleeding.
She attributes her symptoms to spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus, and tomato-based products, as well as stress and work-related anxiety.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.
Her grandmother had esophageal cancer on her mother's side, and her mother had acid reflux, which was managed with medication that coats the stomach lining.
The patient has had an upper endoscopy in the past, but not in the last 10 years.
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: Omeprazole 40 milligrams twice daily.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The physical exam was normal, except for well-healed abdominal surgical scars noted.
The patient had an abdominal surgery as a baby due to twisted intestines.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a several-year history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), which is poorly controlled with omeprazole 40 milligrams twice daily.
Problem list: GERD active; Anxiety active; Seasonal allergies past; Esophageal cancer family history on the mother's side unknown.
Plan:
I prescribed Nexium 40 milligrams twice daily to replace omeprazole.
I will also recommend a procedure called an upper endoscopy (EGD) to look for potential causes of GERD.
I will send a prescription for Nexium 40 milligrams twice daily to the patient's pharmacy on file.

I will also recommend that the patient schedule an appointment for the EGD at the checkout desk and follow up with me in the office to discuss the results and talk about how they are doing with dietary, stress, and medication changes.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in the office for the EGD and to discuss the results and management plan.
